text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Emily Carter  
St. Mary’s Hospital  
Department of Neurology, Praed Street, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Emily Carter,  

Alex Turner, DOB: 14-03-2006, Hospital No: H293847 NHS No. 9845612370  
21 Brookfield Avenue, London, N10 2QJ 

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures, onset in childhood; aetiology and epilepsy classification not established. MRI brain (2023) unremarkable. Routine EEG (2024) non-specific findings without clear epileptiform activity. 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily  
Midazolam 10 mg buccal, as rescue for prolonged events (>5 minutes) 

Plan: 

- Continue levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily.  
- Provide written seizure action plan to patient and parent.  
- Arrange sleep-deprived EEG and repeat serum levels in 3 months.  
- Safety and lifestyle guidance reviewed (sleep hygiene, alcohol moderation, swimming with supervision).  
- Follow-up in adult epilepsy clinic in 6 months, sooner if breakthrough events increase. 

I reviewed Alex Turner in the adult neurology clinic following transition from paediatric services. They attended with a parent, who contributed collateral history. Since moving to adult care earlier this year, they report brief “blanking” spells occurring several times per week, described as short periods of unresponsiveness with loss of conversation thread and a rapid return to baseline, without motor features or post-event confusion. The parent has witnessed some of these episodes and notes that they tend to cluster when the patient is sleep-deprived or under exam stress. There have been no prolonged convulsions in the past 12 months. The patient keeps a seizure diary that records these events as occurring on most weeks, with occasional weeks free. Medication adherence is reported as good, with no significant adverse effects beyond mild irritability that has improved with dose timing adjustments. 

Neurological examination today was normal. Prior investigations include a structurally normal MRI brain (2023) and a routine EEG (2024) showing non-specific slowing without clear epileptiform discharges. In view of the ongoing brief events several times each week and the potential influence of sleep deprivation, I have arranged a sleep-deprived EEG to increase diagnostic yield. We discussed the possibility of a future medication adjustment depending on EEG findings and diary trends; for now, I have advised continuing levetiracetam at the current dose and maintaining the seizure diary. A rescue buccal midazolam plan remains in place for any prolonged event lasting more than 5 minutes, although this has not been required over the last year. 

We reviewed lifestyle and safety considerations, including regular sleep, avoidance of binge drinking, and supervised swimming. The parent is welcome to attend future appointments to provide further collateral history when available. I will see Alex Turner again in six months, with interim review if there is an increase in the frequency or severity of events. 

With best wishes,

entities.text: brief “blanking” spells occurring several times per week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient has brief “blanking” spells occurring several times per week and reiterates ongoing brief events several times each week. The seizure diary notes events on most weeks with occasional weeks free, but the highest explicit frequency given is 'several times per week'. There are no prolonged convulsions in the past 12 months, so the most frequent seizure type is the brief spells. The exact number per week is not specified, so normalize to 'multiple per week' per the allowed format.
seizure_frequency_number: brief “blanking” spells occurring several times per week

flaws.description: Gold (multiple per week) disagrees with Reference (1 per week)
